It's not used directly by any of context managers in python-libarchive-c, but we use it in our project to allow cancelling creation of archive. Since this library is a convenient wrapper around libarchive we think that it's the best place for it (and make this wrapper more complete).
Turns out that if you want to discard the archive you were creating, you can not just stop creating it. Context managers will close the archive, and libarchive will try to fill the missing contents with 000000000000000000..., and that can cause memory overflows. You have to mark the archive as failed (by that function) to tell libarchive that it should give up any work on it.
(see readme for defense against "not atomic")

Sad to disappoint, but no. This only works with either `geventwebsocket.WebSocketServer` as a server or if `handler_class=WebSocketHandler` is passed to `gevent.pywsgi.WSGIServer` constructor.

As far as I know, there seems to be [no common standard](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/gevent/JPRoBdeLTbE) on passing the socket handle to a WSGI application, so every library out there does it differently. So for each server one would need some magic trick, like [uwsgi-websockets](https://github.com/gavinwahl/uwsgi-websockets).

Maybe my understanding is wrong or outdated, though.

1. I'm not sure why `notify` isn't business logic. The SDK doesn't get to specify what is or isn't business logic. Sending or not emails could very much be business logic???

That said I'm fine placing this param anywhere.

2. I thought about that option and then went to the Docs and saw that can_view_path was the only unsupported param.

But thinking long term / broadly... having some sort of policy/pattern in the SDK's API that maps nicely to the actual V2 API would be nice. Something like::
* required params have explicit named-params.
* optionals params are supported via the optional dicts.

Jordan - are you suggesting just body-params would have a dict parameter? What about query params?

Looking at https://developer.box.com/v2.0/reference#add-a-collaboration
* query param `fields`. its optional. The box-sdk doesn't support it.
* query param `notify` the docs don't list it's default. But I *guess* it's optional.
* body param `accessible_by`. It's not labeled as Required. Is that correct
* body param `can_view_path`. Optional, so it could be cover by a dict param

Thus the current boxSDK API "breaks" the model of optional being supported via dicts. So what then... it's just random. Some params are explicit. Others are supported via dict? Maybe that is OK. Maybe not. Or maybe in the move to 2.0 would implement a nice pattern like this? That would mean removing the `notify` param and having a "option_query_params" dict parameter.

This is indeed another way of implementing it, and it does maintain the original signatures! However, it still doesn't give the callers the flexibility to deal with the added scenario differently.

I see your point of adding a transient_error_callback; we're basically assuming people are using the explicit parameter passing in FacebookAdsApiBatch.add, or it'll be a breaking change for them, or unless we add the transient_error_callback to the end of the parameters list. (the FacebookAdsApiBatch.add_request will be fine; we just add the transient_error to the end)

```python
def add(
        self,
        method,
        relative_path,
        params=None,
        headers=None,
        files=None,
        success=None,
        failure=None,
       transient_error=None, #<========= adding here
        request=None,
       transient_error=None, #<========= or adding here
    ):
```
I still prefer adding a transient_error_callback; it's more flexible and thus adding more value to the SDK in the long run. 

We could add a small check in the "add" method; like, testing if "transient_error" parameter type is request object; if it is, we could warn/err out/exception out since it's clearly the caller is using the non-explicit for optional parameters and the new code will break their logic.

@kbourgoin @emmett9001 @rduplain can I bug you guys - and also everyone else whose listening in - for some input on this design choice in particular, before I spend too many hours hammering out the further implementation in the C code?

What this would do is make `produce()` return a future (either a `pykafka.handlers.ResponseFuture` or a backported `concurrent.futures.Future` or something else, if you prefer) and that would just evaluate to `None` if the message made it to the broker or would raise an appropriate exception from `pykafka.exceptions` if not (ie if max_retries got exceeded for some messages, and what went wrong). And for `self._synchronous=True` it could then just evaluate the future as part of `produce()`.

(I'd also volunteer to write the pullreq to make that work the same way in `pykafka.Producer` if we like it.)

Now the question: I'd like this futures-based design mostly because it is easy to reason about from a user perspective - 1 message in, 1 future out, and you can check it or ignore it, whatever suits - but I'd like your thoughts about the practicality of it in a bigger application. Any and all alternative interface suggestions very welcome.

(I'll note that `concurrent.futures` can also facilitate a [callback interface][0], if needs be...)

[0]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.Future.add_done_callback

After reading https://packaging.python.org/discussions/install-requires-vs-requirements/ I understand that requirements.txt and install_requires are for different things. `requirements.txt` is for the "concrete" requirements, while `install_requires` is for the "abstract" requirements. The primary difference seems to be that one specifies exact versions to pin to, while the other provides more general restrictions.

The others reason why I didn't do it the same way was because even without the version numbers, the lists are different. The only package we require to run is `requests`, yet `requirements.txt` contains entries for all of `requests` dependencies too.

What do you think?

In this particular case, that other solution is actually documented in [official document](https://docs.python.org/3/library/platform.html#cross-platform). And it IS the correct value because, as a scripting language running inside its interpretor, you can only know what architecture (64-bit or 32-bit) your interpretor supports. You don't know what the OS actually supports (and that doesn't mean anything to your current scripting environment anyway).

For example, I'm now using Windows 10, 64 bit, but install a 32-bit Python 3.5. Both the `platform.architecture()[0]` and `'x64' if sys.maxsize > 2**32 else 'x86'` give me a "x86" value, which is considered correct.

> We shouldn't introduce new concepts
> Custom events and triggers seem to add more to the framework


Totally on board with this. I think this warrants an exception because:

1. I see it as the last thing we need to add. It's important that we explore the use-cases before implementing this. We shouldn't add this unless it is the last thing.
2. I see this as "we substitute a bunch of tailor-fit concepts by a generic one". This removes the need for the `when_file_changed` decorator and it removes the need for the special behavior of the `config.changed` flag.
3. Layer devs will be able to use this to create their own `when_file_changed`-like flags. Layer devs continue making smarter layers without needing to add additional stuff to reactive.

> It's very similar to the preflight decorator

That's correct. Our reasoning against preflight is that 1) it is to tied to the inner workings of reactive and hooks and 2) it breaks the concept of "flags define when a handler runs". Having something happen "before any handler runs" is clearly a much needed feature. However, that "something" shouldn't be a handler.

> You can't determine the order that things run in

The order in which handlers run should be determined by the flags and only the flags. That's why we have them. "Layers are snippets of reusable ops code and flags are used for coordination between these layers". This is why I don't want the `trigger` decorator from your comment. We shouldn't add a second way to order handlers. We just need to make the current method a little bit more powerful. The only thing that a trigger can do is change a flag. In that sense they're a lot less powerful than `preflight`. By design. The order of handlers is still coordinated by the flags. We just have gained the ability to connect flags.

Do we want to permit only the last parameter to be variadic? If we allow a parameter other than the last one to be variadic (still restricting the number of variadic parameters to be one), then the association between the index of an actual parameter and the index of the corresponding formal parameter is more complex. For parameters before the optional one, it is a direct match. For parameters after the optional one, we need to count from the back ... that is, the k-th parameter from the end in the actual parameter list will correspond to the k-th parameter from the end in the formal parameter list.

@linkerzhang if you want to implement quantized operators/functions on top of ConvInteger/MatMulInteger, it needs to be the same. beware that backends which implement quantized operators directly may use a different internal representation (e.g. store everything as 9-bit signed integers instead of 8-bit unsigned + zero point). using the same `uint8` tensor with different `zero_point` values would make it impossible to use quantized operators with backends using any representation, but canonical `uint8` + `zero_point` + `scale`.
